schema_note: naive_reliability_score = simple rolling on-time-delivery rate over last N cycles. pressure_adjusted_score = same, but failures during demand-surge / high-load cycles are weighted more heavily, because reliability under normal load is not evidence of reliability under stress. The gap between the two scores is itself a signal an agent must surface, not average away.

suppliers.name: AgroFresh Vegetables
suppliers.supplies_ingredient: Green leafy vegetables (spinach/methi) - recipe-locked input for PRG-002 (Tier 1)

history.note: Missed 53% of committed volume specifically on the one cycle load exceeded steady-state. This is the ONLY data point that matters for tomorrow's surge-day forecast, but a naive rolling average buries it under two clean cycles.

suppliers.flag: RECENCY_VS_PRESSURE - naive average (83%) reads as reliable; performance under the one load condition matching tomorrow's forecast (surge) was 47% of commitment. Treat as high-risk for any surge-day plan, not as a reliable supplier having an off day.

suppliers.name: Rajasthan Fortified Mills
suppliers.supplies_ingredient: Fortified rice - shared recipe input for PRG-002 and PRG-005

history.note: Delivery itself was fine - the Day2 problem was downstream allocation, not supply. See decision_log C4 Day2 reversal.

suppliers.flag: SUPPLY_NOT_THE_RISK - this supplier is clean. The C4 incident was caused by two Tier-1 recipes drawing on one unreserved stock pool, which no supplier-side metric would ever surface.
